title: BGP Update Volume Tracking
dataSources: `index=thousandeyes_metrics sourcetype=cisco:thousandeyes:metric` from the Tests Stream — Metrics input in `ta_cisco_thousandeyes` (Splunkbase 7719). The OTel v2 metric `bgp.updates.count` reports the number of raw BGP UPDATE messages observed per collection interval per monitor. BGP UPDATEs include both announcements (new/changed routes) and withdrawals (removed routes). A single path change (UC-5.9.9) may generate multiple BGP UPDATEs as the new path propagates and ISPs re-advertise. Resource attributes: `thousandeyes.test.type` (`bgp`), `thousandeyes.monitor.name`, `thousandeyes.monitor.location`, `network.prefix`.
app: `ta_cisco_thousandeyes` — Cisco ThousandEyes App for Splunk ([Splunkbase 7719](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/7719)), Cisco Supported.
spl:
`stream_index` thousandeyes.test.type="bgp"
| timechart span=1h sum(bgp.updates.count) as bgp_updates by network.prefix
description: Tracks the raw volume of BGP UPDATE messages (announcements + withdrawals) observed across ThousandEyes' BGP monitors. While UC-5.9.9 counts logical path changes, this UC counts the underlying UPDATE message volume — a higher-fidelity signal for detecting route flapping, which can cause router CPU exhaustion and BGP session resets on routers processing the flapping prefix.
value: BGP UPDATE storms have a cascading effect beyond just routing changes. When a prefix flaps rapidly (withdraw-announce-withdraw-announce), every router in the path must process each UPDATE, consuming CPU and memory. If the flap rate is high enough, it can trigger route damping (which suppresses the prefix entirely for 30+ minutes), cause BGP session timeouts on overloaded routers (tearing down peering sessions with collateral damage to other prefixes), or push router CPU past capacity (degrading forwarding performance for all traffic). By tracking UPDATE volume in Splunk and alerting on anomalies, the network team can detect flapping before it causes collateral damage — and the Security team can detect DDoS attacks that manipulate BGP to disrupt routing infrastructure.
implementation: Uses the same Tests Stream — Metrics data as UC-5.9.8 and UC-5.9.9. The `bgp.updates.count` metric is reported alongside `bgp.reachability` and `bgp.path_changes.count` in the same BGP test events. Set alerts when update volume exceeds 3 standard deviations from the 7-day baseline.
visualization: (1) Line chart: `timechart span=1h sum(bgp.updates.count) as updates by network.prefix` — shows UPDATE volume trends. Spikes stand out against a flat baseline. (2) Single value: total updates in the last hour vs 7-day average (red if > 3× average). (3) Correlation panel: overlay UPDATE volume with path change count (UC-5.9.9) and reachability (UC-5.9.8) for the same prefix. A spike in UPDATEs with stable path changes suggests flapping (repeated changes along the same paths). A spike in UPDATEs with many path changes suggests convergence (routes shifting to new stable paths).

references.title: BGP UPDATE storms and their impact — NANOG presentation

knownFalsePositives:
**Initial BGP test setup or monitor reconnection.** When a monitor reconnects to its BGP peer (e.g., after maintenance or a software update), it receives a full routing table dump, which generates a burst of UPDATEs for all monitored prefixes. Distinguish by checking whether the spike affects all prefixes from a single monitor simultaneously and whether it resolves within 1–2 collection intervals.

**Internet-wide BGP events.** Submarine cable cuts, large ISP peering changes, or major cloud provider routing updates can cause global UPDATE spikes. If the spike affects all monitored prefixes equally, it's a backbone event. Check bgpstream.com, ThousandEyes Outages, and ISP status pages.

**Prefix aggregation/deaggregation changes.** When your network team changes prefix aggregation (e.g., withdrawing a /24 and replacing with a covering /22, or vice versa), monitors see withdraw+announce UPDATEs. Correlate with change management.

**BGP graceful restart.** Some routers send route-refresh or soft-reconfiguration UPDATEs during planned maintenance. These are benign but show as UPDATE volume spikes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• All prerequisites from UC-5.9.8 apply — BGP tests configured, Tests Stream — Metrics enabled, BGP data flowing.
• **Baseline period:** Like UC-5.9.9, allow 7 days of data before setting alert thresholds. BGP UPDATE volumes have a wide normal range depending on the prefix and the routing landscape around it.
• **Understand the difference between UPDATEs and path changes:** A single path change may generate 1 UPDATE, or it may generate many UPDATEs as the change propagates through the BGP mesh and multiple monitors observe it. Conversely, a route flap generates many UPDATEs (alternating withdraw/announce) but may show as only 1–2 path changes (the same two paths alternating). This is why both UC-5.9.9 and UC-5.9.10 exist — they measure different facets of routing instability.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Same as UC-5.9.8. No additional configuration.

Verify:
```spl
index=thousandeyes_metrics thousandeyes.test.type="bgp" earliest=-1h
| stats sum(bgp.updates.count) as total_updates by network.prefix
```

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
**Trending search (dashboard):**
```spl
`stream_index` thousandeyes.test.type="bgp"
| timechart span=1h sum(bgp.updates.count) as bgp_updates by network.prefix
```

**Alert search (anomaly detection):**
```spl
`stream_index` thousandeyes.test.type="bgp" earliest=-1h
| stats sum(bgp.updates.count) as hourly_updates by network.prefix
| where hourly_updates > 50
| sort -hourly_updates
```

**Understanding this SPL**

`sum(bgp.updates.count)` — total UPDATE messages across all monitors. This is a volume metric — higher numbers indicate more BGP activity. Normal for most prefixes: 0–10 UPDATEs per hour. During a route flap or convergence event: 50–500+.

`where hourly_updates > 50` — starting threshold. Tune based on your 7-day baseline. For a stable, well-announced /24 with a single upstream, > 20 may be anomalous. For a multihomed /16 with many upstream paths, > 100 is the appropriate threshold.

**Flap detection variant** (distinguishes flapping from convergence):
```spl
`stream_index` thousandeyes.test.type="bgp" earliest=-1h
| stats sum(bgp.updates.count) as updates sum(bgp.path_changes.count) as path_changes by network.prefix
| eval ratio = if(path_changes>0, round(updates/path_changes, 1), updates)
| eval pattern = case(
    updates>50 AND ratio>5, "ROUTE FLAP — many updates per path change (oscillating between same paths)",
    updates>50 AND ratio<=5, "CONVERGENCE — updates from many monitors observing path changes",
    updates>20 AND path_changes=0, "UPDATE STORM — updates without logical path changes (possible aggregation/policy change)",
    1=1, "NORMAL")
| where pattern!="NORMAL"
| sort -updates
```
The ratio of UPDATEs to path changes reveals the nature of the instability: a high ratio means the same paths are flapping; a low ratio means paths are genuinely shifting.

**Scheduling:** Dashboard: auto-refresh every 15 minutes. Alert: cron `0 * * * *`, time range `-1h to now`. Throttle by `network.prefix` for 4 hours.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Baseline establishment.** After 7 days, compute: `| stats avg(bgp_updates) as avg_updates stdev(bgp_updates) as stdev_updates by network.prefix`. Record the average and standard deviation for each prefix. Set alert threshold at `avg + 3 * stdev`.

(b) **Correlation with path changes.** Plot UPDATEs alongside path changes for the same prefix. During normal operations, both should be near zero. During a routing event, both should spike (with UPDATEs typically higher than path changes).

(c) **Zero UPDATEs is normal.** A completely stable prefix produces 0 UPDATEs. Only flag zero as a problem if you expect routing changes (e.g., during a migration).

Step 4 — Operationalize
**Dashboard** (add as a panel in the UC-5.9.8 "BGP Prefix Health" dashboard):
• Timechart: BGP UPDATEs per hour per prefix, with a reference line at the 3σ threshold.
• Correlation: overlay UPDATEs, path changes, and reachability on the same timechart (triple Y-axis or three stacked panels).
• Classification table: prefix | updates | path changes | ratio | pattern (FLAP/CONVERGENCE/NORMAL).

**Runbook** (owner: Network Engineering / Security):
1. **ROUTE FLAP detected (high update-to-change ratio):** A prefix is oscillating between paths. Check whether your border router is the source (flapping interface, unstable IGP-to-BGP redistribution) or an upstream ISP. If your router, stabilize the interface. If upstream, contact the ISP. Consider enabling BGP route flap damping if not already enabled.
2. **CONVERGENCE detected (updates matching path changes):** The routing landscape is shifting — expected during ISP maintenance or backbone events. Monitor for reachability impact.
3. **UPDATE STORM without path changes:** Possible BGP soft-reconfiguration, route-refresh, or a policy change that causes mass re-advertisement without changing the best path. Usually benign but monitor router CPU.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **`bgp.updates.count` always 0 but `bgp.path_changes.count` shows activity** — The two metrics may be named differently in your app version, or UPDATE counting may not be enabled. Check `| fieldsummary | search field=bgp*` for available BGP metric fields.

• **UPDATE counts much higher than path changes** — This is expected. A single path change can generate many UPDATEs as it propagates across the BGP mesh. The ratio of updates-to-changes gives you the propagation factor.

• **All common troubleshooting** — See UC-5.9.8 and UC-5.9.1 Step 5.
grandmaExplanation: We count how many routing messages the internet exchanges about our addresses. A sudden flood of messages means the routers are arguing about how to reach us, and that argument can slow down or break the connection for our users.
